retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: EGFR and ErbB4 are fully functional receptors that bind ligands and undergo autophosphorylation (ie, tyrosine kinase activation). ErbB2 has no known ligand but is the preferred dimerization partner for EGFR, ErbB3, and ErbB4. ErbB3 has no intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity but can transduce signals through interaction with kinase-active receptors such as EGFR, ErbB2, and ErbB4. Seven known ligands bind and activate EGFRs: EGF, transforming growth factor alpha (TGFa), Amphiregulin (Areg), Betacellulin (Btc), Epiregulin (Ereg), heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor (HB-EGF), and Epigen (Epgn). Tyrosine phosphorylation of receptors stimulates various signaling pathways, including Ras/MAPK, PLCγ1/PKC, PI(3)kinase/Akt, and STAT pathways. Aberrant expression of EGF ligands and hyperactivation of EGFRs due to mutations are implicated in many human diseases ranging from various human cancers to psoriasis, Alzheimer disease, and schizophrenia. The pathologies associated with EGFRs have led to novel treatment modalities, including monoclonal antibodies designed to inhibit EGFR signaling. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Cenegermin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Upon binding to TrkA, NGF initiates intracellular activation of several signaling pathways, including mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), Ras, phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K), cell division control protein 42 (Cdc42), and phospholipase-C gamma (PLC-γ). However, the precise mechanism is not fully elucidated. The rhNGF directly acts on corneal epithelial cells to preserve limbal epithelial stem cell potential and lacrimal gland receptors, thereby promoting tear production. Moreover, the rhNGF has demonstrated the capacity to accelerate nerve fiber density recovery, actively supporting corneal reinnervation. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Phospholipase A2 -- Function -- Ca-Independent PLA
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers have studied the functions of Ca-independent PLA as playing an essential role in normal cell homeostasis. There have been implications regarding its role in the promotion of cell cycle progression and, paradoxically, apoptosis of cells depending on the target cell. Other studied functions of the enzyme include the proper formation of bones, glucose-dependent insulin secretion, maturation of sperm, proper function of skeletal and smooth muscle, and neuroaxonal regeneration in response to injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: S1PRs (S1PR1, S1PR2, and S1PR3) are highly expressed within the brain, heart, spleen, lung, thymus, and skeletal muscle cells. [5] The S1PR signaling pathway is an essential regulator of lymphocyte trafficking from lymph nodes to circulation. [8] Additionally, this pathway mediates several nonimmune essential functions, such as vascular and neurogenic angiogenesis, vessel permeability, basal vascular tone, dendritic cell migration, and bacteremia. [9] [8] [10] [11] [12] S1PR1 and S1PR3 are expressed in the membranes of the atrium myocytes and provide a chronotropic effect on the heart. [5] S1PR4 is involved in immunological aspects of carcinogenesis by regulating the proliferation and survival of natural killer cells, while both S1PR4 and S1PR5 modulate innate immune cell response. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The EGF ligands that bind the EGFRs include EGF, transforming growth factor-α (TGF-α), heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor (HB-EGF), amphiregulin, betacellulin, epiregulin, and epigen. Each ligand is characterized by a consensus sequence consisting of 6 spatially conserved cysteine residues (CX7 CX4–5 CX10–13 CXCX8 C) that form 3 intramolecular disulfide bonds. This consensus sequence is the EGF motif and is critical for binding with the EGFRs.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: EGFR is a 170 kD single polypeptide chain of 1186 amino acids expressed on the surface of most human cells. This receptor is made up of extracellular, intracellular, and transmembrane regions. The extracellular region facilitates ligand binding. These ligands include EGF, HB-EGF, TGF-α, amphiregulin, epiregulin, betacellulin, and epigen. These ligands share a conserved motif of 6 cysteines. The intracellular carboxy-terminal region is a tyrosine kinase. This tyrosine kinase autophosphorylates specific intracellular tyrosine residues, with Tyr-1173 as the major autophosphorylation site. The transmembrane region is a single hydrophobic anchor that transverses the cell membrane.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Phospholipase A2 -- Pathophysiology -- Secretory PLA
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of ARDS and asthma involve two mechanisms related to normal respiratory physiology. Normal expression of secretory PLA results in increased leukotrienes, which serve as potent chemokines, resulting in leukocyte attraction and subsequent release of proinflammatory cytokines. This role has been established in the pathogenesis of asthma. Another mechanism that contributes more to the pathophysiology of ARDS involves secretory PLA degradation of the lung surfactants phosphatidylcholine and phosphatidylglycerol, resulting in further respiratory inflammation and alveolar collapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Upon EGFR phosphorylation, the adaptor proteins Grb2 and SOS bind directly or indirectly via the adaptor molecule SHC to specific phosphorylation sites on the receptor. This interaction leads to Ras-GDP recruitment, resulting in Ras activation (Ras-GTP). Ras-GTP activates Raf-1, which results in activation of the mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPK). Activated MAPKs are then translocated into the nucleus, phosphorylating specific transcription factors involved in cell proliferation. EGFR can also activate phospholipase C gamma (PLCG), leading to protein kinase C (PKC) activation. PLCG hydrolyses phosphatidylinositol 4,5-diphosphate to produce inositol 1,3,5-triphosphate (IP3), essential for intracellular calcium release, and 1,2-diacylglycerol (DAG), a cofactor in PKC activation. PKC activation can activate MAPK and c-Jun NH2-terminal kinase (JNK).

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod's molecular structure resembles a bioactive lipid called sphingosine, which is produced in the cell membrane and transported extracellularly to modulate a series of biological responses. Sphingosine receptors are a group of 5 G-protein–coupled receptors expressed throughout the body and are associated with various biological processes. [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, G Protein Coupled Receptors -- Fundamentals -- Signaling by GPCRs
retrieved_docs.content: GPCRs activate various intracellular signaling, including generating second messengers such as cyclic AMP and inositol phospholipids. GPCRs that stimulate the production of cyclic AMP are often coupled to the stimulatory G protein (Gs), which activates adenylyl cyclase and increases cyclic AMP levels. However, binding an inhibitory G protein (Gi) to a GPCR can inhibit cyclic AMP synthesis. Both Gs and Gi are targets of bacterial toxins. Cholera toxin interferes with the activity of the α subunit by ADP-ribosylation, causing it to remain active. Sustained and elevated concentration of cycle AMP in intestinal epithelial cells results in diarrhea. Pertussis toxin that causes pertussis (whooping cough) also can hydrolyze ADP-ribosylation of the α subunit of Gi, which inhibits its binding with GPCR, which results in the α subunit to remain in the GDP-bound state and makes it unable to regulate downstream signaling events. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Phospholipase C breaks down PIP (Phosphoinositol diphosphate) into IP (inositol triphosphate) and DAG (Diacyl Glycerol).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fasting -- Mechanism -- Lipid
retrieved_docs.content: The breakdown of triglycerides begins with the activation of hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL). This enzyme is stimulated by glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, and growth hormone all of which have increased plasma levels during fasting. [11] Each of these hormones activates HSL through a different pathway. Glucagon and epinephrine bind to adenylyl cyclase (on the cell membrane) creating cyclic AMP. Cyclic AMP activates protein kinase A (PKA), which in turn activates HSL. Cortisol binds to glucocorticoid receptor alpha (GR-alpha) located in the cytosol of the cell. Activation of GR-alpha increases transcription of the protein angiopoietin-like 4 (Angptl4). This protein directly stimulates cyclic AMP-dependent PKA signaling which tells HSL to begin lipolysis. [12] Growth hormone turns on HSL through the phospholipase C (PLC) pathway. PLC activates protein kinase C (PKC) which can either directly or indirectly stimulate HSL. The indirect pathway involves PKC phosphorylating MAPK/ERK kinase (MEK). MEK phosphorylates extracellular signal-related kinase (ERK) which directly phosphorylates HSL.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: MAPK/ERK: Mitogen-activated protein kinase/extracellular signal-regulated kinase 1/2 PI3K/Akt/mTOR: Phosphoinositide 3 kinase/Akt/mammalian (or mechanistic) target of rapamycin PLCG1/PKC: Phospholipase C-gamma1/protein kinase C

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod requires in vivo phosphorylation to activate S1PR. [15] [16] The drug binds nonselectively to S1PR, primarily receptors 1, 3, 4, and 5. [5] This binding leads to receptor internalization and recycling, except for S1PR1, which undergoes irreversible degradation within the formed endosomes. [1] Continued administration causes a stepwise reduction in available receptors and desensitization. [1] Fingolimod Fingolimod exhibits a high affinity for S1PR1, which is prominently expressed in lymphocytes. [17] [5] Activation of S1PR1 by sphingosine-1-phosphate stimulates lymphocyte migration, thereby promoting inflammation. Therefore, inhibiting S1PR1 with fingolimod reduces circulating lymphocytes and limits the inflammatory response. Fingolimod affects T- and B-cell trafficking, as well as oligodendrocytes, where S1PR5 receptors are expressed. [1] Moreover, fingolimod crosses the blood-brain barrier, helping to preserve its integrity and potentially reducing brain edema. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Stuttering Priapism (Recurrent or Intermittent Priapism) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The molecular mechanism thought to cause stuttering priapism involves reduced cavernosal nitric oxide in the endothelial cells, which downregulates phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE-5) and reduces RhoA/Rho-kinase activity while increasing adenosine and opiorphin levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cerebral Autoregulation -- Mechanism -- Endothelial Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, endothelial tissue begets a gamut of signals that affect vascular tone. The endothelium secretes vasodilators like nitric oxide (NO) and vasoconstrictors like thromboxane A2 and endothelin-1 in a paracrine manner. Further, as an interesting bedside-to-bench endeavor, researchers have looked at the ability of statins to regulate autoregulation. In more detail, statins can upregulate nitric oxide synthase, causing cerebral artery dilation and increased cerebral blood flow. [17] [18] This mechanism occurs by inhibiting small G-proteins known as Rho and Rac. Rho negatively regulates endothelial nitric oxide synthase. Statins inhibit Rho GTPase activity via inhibition of a process known as geranylgeranylation, a type of prenylation that ultimately confers nitric oxide synthase upregulation.

retrieved_docs.content: terbutalin, tretochinol and tulobuterol. Formoterol and salmeterol carry a specific contraindication in acute disease, indicating they should not be used during an acute exacerbation. Several agents—carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, isoetharin and Formoterol—are also listed as selective beta‑2‑adrenergic agonists for systemic use, allowing systemic β₂ effects such as uterine relaxation and peripheral vasodilation. Non‑selective beta‑adrenergic agonists include metaproterenol, which appears both as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor inhalant and as a systemic agent; it stimulates β₁ and β₂ receptors, providing combined cardiac and bronchodilatory actions, and is additionally classified as a tocolytic for inhibition of uterine contractions. All systemic β‑adrenergic agents belong to the larger category adrenergics for systemic use. Within the systemic selective β₂‑agonist subgroup, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, terbutalin, tretochinol and tulobuterol are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 885
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class of drugs that selectively stimulate β2‑adrenergic receptors throughout the body, producing smooth‑muscle relaxation and bronchodilation. The agents fenoterol, hexoprenalin, pirbuterol, reproterol, tretochinol and tulobuterol are all members of this class. By activating systemic β2 receptors, these medications are used to manage obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and they share a common mechanism of action while differing in individual pharmacokinetic properties and clinical formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” (a subclass of “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” within “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS”) comprises systemic agents used for severe asthma and other obstructive airway conditions, including amlexanox, eprozinol, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab. Within the inhalant domain, the parent class “Adrenergics, inhalants” is divided into three subclasses: (1) “Alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants,” (2) “Non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants,” represented by metaproterenol (also listed as a systemic agent), and (3) “Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants,” which include formoterol, bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, indacaterol, isoetharin, klenbuterol, olodaterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, salmeterol, terbutalin, tretochinol and tulobuterol. All selective β2‑agonists produce rapid bronchodilation in asthma and COPD

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 599
retrieved_docs.content: The drug class “Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic” comprises several specific agents, including carbuterol, hexoprenalin, isoetharin, klenbuterol, reproterol, and tretochinol. All of these medications share the characteristic of selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors throughout the body, which produces bronchodilation and other systemic β2‑mediated effects. Recognizing that each of these compounds is a member of the systemic selective β2‑agonist class helps clinicians understand their common pharmacologic actions, therapeutic uses (such as relief of bronchospasm), and potential side‑effect profiles, thereby supporting more informed prescribing and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1352
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta-2-adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic is a pharmacologic class that includes fenoterol, hexoprenalin, klenbuterol, reproterol, and tulobuterol. All of these agents are members of this class and share the characteristic of selectively activating beta-2 adrenergic receptors when administered systemically, which underlies their use in conditions requiring bronchodilation and smooth‑muscle relaxation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class that includes hexoprenalin, isoetharin, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, reproterol and tretochinol, each of which is a member of this group. All of these agents act systemically to selectively stimulate β2‑adrenergic receptors, producing bronchodilation and smooth‑muscle relaxation that are useful in the treatment of obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By sharing the same mechanism of selective β2 activation, these drugs provide clinicians with multiple therapeutic options that differ in pharmacokinetic properties, potency and duration of action, allowing individualized patient care based on specific clinical needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1137
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the agents carbuterol, fenoterol, isoetharin, prokaterol, reproterol, tretochinol and tulobuterol. All of these drugs are members of this class and share the characteristic of selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors when administered systemically, providing bronchodilatory effects that are useful in the management of obstructive airway diseases. Recognizing that each of these compounds belongs to the same therapeutic group helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential indications, and safety profiles, facilitating appropriate selection and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 557
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class of systemic bronchodilators that act by selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors in airway smooth muscle. The agents carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, pirbuterol, reproterol and tretochinol are all members of this class. By binding to β2 receptors, each of these drugs promotes relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle, thereby improving airflow in conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Their systemic formulation distinguishes them from inhaled β2‑agonists and may be selected when broader distribution or alternative routes of administration are required. Understanding that carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, pirbuterol, reproterol and tretochinol share this common mechanism helps clinicians choose appropriate systemic β2‑agonist therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: Salmeterol shares the same contraindication. A subset of these agents—carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, and isoetharin—are additionally classified as selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists for systemic use, providing widespread β2 stimulation when administered orally or parenterally. Metaproterenol occupies a distinct niche as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist; it is available as both an inhalant and a systemic drug, stimulates β1 and β2 receptors, and belongs to the non‑selective beta‑adrenergic agonist class for systemic use, itself a subset of the broader adrenergics for systemic use. Systemic selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists also comprise klenbuterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, terbutalin, tretochinol, and tulobuterol. Bambuterol is mapped to terbutalin, indicating therapeutic equivalence within this subgroup. Combination products that merge adrenergic bronchodilators with anticholinergic agents—and, in some formulations, with corticosteroids—are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the agents fenoterol, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, reproterol, tretochinol and tulobuterol. Each of these drugs is a systemic, selective agonist of the β2‑adrenergic receptor, and they share the common mechanism of stimulating β2 receptors in smooth muscle to produce bronchodilation. Because of this shared activity, the agents are employed in the treatment of obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, providing relief of bronchospasm and improving airflow. Their classification as systemic selective β2‑agonists underscores their similar therapeutic role and pharmacodynamic profile despite differences in individual chemical structure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1203
retrieved_docs.content: The class of systemic selective β2‑adrenoreceptor agonists comprises the drugs fenoterol, hexoprenalin, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol and tulobuterol. Each of these agents is a member of the selective β2‑adrenoreceptor agonist group and is administered systemically to achieve targeted β2‑adrenergic stimulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: and as a systemic beta‑2 agonist. Formoterol, like salmeterol, is contraindicated in patients with akutní nemoc (acute disease). The class of selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants comprises a wide array of agents: bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, indacaterol, isoetharin, klenbuterol, olodaterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, salmeterol, terbutalin and tulobuterol. The systemic subclass “Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic” includes many of the same compounds—carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, isoetharin, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, terbutalin and tulobuterol—demonstrating that these drugs are employed in both inhaled and systemic formulations. Reproterol is mapped from theofylin, indicating that theofylin is a structural precursor or analogue of reproterol. Bambuterol is mapped to terbutalin, reflecting its role as a pro‑drug or metabolite related to terbutalin. Overall, the knowledge base integrates hierarchical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1059
retrieved_docs.content: The systemic selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist class comprises the drugs fenoterol, hexoprenalin, isoetharin, pirbuterol, reproterol and tulobuterol, each of which is a member of this pharmacologic group. These agents share the characteristic of selectively stimulating beta‑2 adrenergic receptors when administered systemically, a property that underlies their therapeutic use in conditions such as asthma and other disorders requiring bronchodilation. Recognizing that all six medications belong to the same selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate contexts for systemic administration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
retrieved_docs.content: The community classifies adrenergic inhalants into mechanistic subclasses—alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants, non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants (e.g., the metaproterenol group), and selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants. The selective beta‑2 subclass comprises Formoterol, bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, indacaterol, isoetharin, klenbuterol, olodaterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, salmeterol, terbutalin, tretochinol, and tulobuterol; Formoterol is also listed as a systemic selective beta‑2 agonist and is contraindicated in patients with acute disease. Systemic non‑selective beta‑agonists such as metaproterenol (used as a tocolytic) belong to the broader “adrenergics for systemic use” class, and systemic selective beta‑2 agents include the same inhaled compounds, with the prodrug bambuterol mapped to terbutalin. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, indacaterol, isoetharin, klenbuterol, olodaterol, pirbuterol and prokaterol, all of which selectively stimulate airway β2‑adrenergic receptors to provide rapid bronchodilation in asthma and COPD. The inhaled subgroup of selective β2‑agonists further includes reproterol, salmeterol, terbutalin, tretochinol and tulobuterol, while the systemic subgroup encompasses carbuterol, fenoterol, hexoprenalin, isoetharin, klenbuterol, pirbuterol, prokaterol, reproterol, terbutalin, tretochinol and tulobuterol, reflecting the availability of these agents in oral, intravenous or intramuscular formulations for situations where inhalation is unsuitable or additional systemic β2‑stimulation is required. Reproterol and terbutalin appear in both inhaled and systemic categories, indicating multiple dosage forms that allow clinicians to tailor the route of administration. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1654
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonists, systemic constitute a pharmacologic class of drugs that act selectively on β2‑adrenergic receptors when administered systemically. The agents hexoprenalin, isoetharin, klenbuterol, reproterol, and tulobuterol are all members of this class. As systemic β2‑agonists, each of these compounds shares the characteristic of stimulating β2 receptors to produce bronchodilation, uterine relaxation, and other smooth‑muscle effects, making them useful in the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and preterm labor. Their classification together underscores their similar mechanism of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profile, which is important for clinicians when selecting and managing systemic β2‑adrenergic therapy.
